question: Our evaluation is slipping and the offer validity period will run out before we can award. What are our options if some suppliers agree to extend and others do not?
must_include_concepts: extension request to responsive offerors
what happens if not all agree
what happens if validity expires before award
should_avoid: answering only with the normal validity-period rule
evaluation_focus: Timeline-sensitive Buyer’s Guide workflow.
required_claims: Canada may request in writing that all responsive offerors extend the offer validity period at least three days before it ends.
If all responsive offerors agree to the extension, the evaluation can continue under the extended validity period.
If not all responsive offerors agree, Canada may either continue evaluating only the offers from those who accepted the extension or cancel the solicitation.
If the offer acceptance period expires before contract award, the solicitation must be reissued, and any contract awarded after expiry is treated as a sole-source contract.
forbidden_claims: If the offer validity period expires before contract award, Canada can still award under the original solicitation without reissuing or sole-source justification.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says Canada may request a written extension from all responsive offerors at least three days before the validity period ends. If all responsive offerors agree, the evaluation can continue under the extended period. If not all agree, Canada may either continue evaluating the offers from those who accepted the extension or cancel the solicitation. If the validity period expires before contract award, the solicitation must be reissued, and any post-expiry award is treated as a sole-source contract.

claim_evidence.claim: Canada may request a written extension from all responsive offerors at least three days before the end of the offer validity period.
If all responsive offerors accept the extension, the evaluation may continue under the extended period.
If all responsive offerors do not accept the extension, Canada may continue evaluating only those offers whose offerors accepted the extension or cancel the solicitation.
If the offer validity period expires before contract award, the solicitation must be reissued and any post-expiry award is considered sole-source.

question: If a supplier is under vendor performance and corrective measures, what do I have to check before I keep evaluating or award anything?
must_include_concepts: VIM or VPCM checks
timing at closing and before award
required action if a relevant VPCM exists
should_avoid: replacing VPCM with broader integrity-screening language
evaluation_focus: Niche operational screening step that buyers may ask about directly.
required_claims: The buyer must check the VIM system for a relevant VPCM at closing for competitive solicitations or before contract for sole sourcing, and recheck before contract award or issuing a standing offer or supply arrangement.
The buyer must not evaluate offers from an offeror subject to a relevant VPCM and must set aside any standing offer or supply arrangement for a vendor under a relevant VPCM.
The buyer may reject offers due to a relevant VPCM at any point up to contract award or issuance of a standing offer or supply arrangement.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says the buyer must check VIM for a relevant VPCM at closing for competitive files or before contract for sole source, and recheck before contract award or issuing a standing offer or supply arrangement. A buyer must not evaluate offers from an offeror subject to a relevant VPCM, must set aside any standing offer or supply arrangement for that vendor, and may reject offers for a relevant VPCM at any point up to award or issuance.

claim_evidence.claim: Check VIM for a relevant VPCM at closing for competitive solicitations or before contract for sole sourcing, and recheck before award or issuance.
Do not evaluate offers from an offeror subject to a relevant VPCM and set aside any standing offer or supply arrangement for such a vendor.
Offers may be rejected due to a relevant VPCM at any point up to contract award or issuance of a standing offer or supply arrangement.

question: A company sent a statement of capabilities in response to our ACAN. How do we review it, what happens if it actually meets the requirement, and what do we need to keep on file?
must_include_concepts: review against ACAN criteria
move to competitive process if it meets requirements
independent review and outcome documentation
should_avoid: abstaining when the page contains the answer across adjacent chunks
evaluation_focus: Multi-chunk same-section packing case that mirrors a real buyer workflow.
required_claims: All statements of capabilities received within the ACAN timeframe must be reviewed against the ACAN criteria, applying the same standards to the pre-identified business.
If a statement sufficiently meets the ACAN requirements, the buyer should proceed with a competitive process.
Rejected statements of capabilities must undergo a separate review by a higher authority, and rejected businesses must be formally notified in writing with reasons.
The procurement file must document the process followed in accepting or rejecting statements of capabilities, including any independent review and the final assessment or outcome.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says statements of capabilities received within the ACAN timeframe must be reviewed against the ACAN criteria using the same standards as for the pre-identified business. If a statement sufficiently meets the requirements, the buyer should proceed with a competitive process. Rejected statements must receive a separate higher-authority review and rejected businesses must be notified in writing with reasons. The procurement file must document how statements were accepted or rejected, including any independent review and the final assessment or outcome.

claim_evidence.claim: Statements of capabilities received within the ACAN timeframe must be reviewed against the ACAN criteria using the same standards as the pre-identified business.
If a statement sufficiently meets the ACAN requirements, the process should move to a competitive process.
Rejected statements must undergo a separate review by a higher authority and rejected businesses must be formally notified in writing with reasons.
The procurement file must document the acceptance or rejection process, including any independent review and the final assessment or outcome.

question: What is the actual contractual difference between a standing offer and a supply arrangement? When does Canada become legally bound under each one?
must_include_concepts: standing offer is not itself a contract
call-up creates contract
no contract at supply arrangement stage
contract exists when contract is awarded under the arrangement
should_avoid: describing both as generic supplier lists
evaluation_focus: Cross-page synthesis question that a real client might ask in plain language.
required_claims: A standing offer is not itself a contract; Canada becomes contractually bound when a call-up is made, and each call-up is a separate contract.
There is no contractual obligation between Canada and the supplier at the supply arrangement stage.
Canada becomes contractually bound under a supply arrangement when an individual contract is awarded under the arrangement, and each resulting contract is a separate binding contract.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide distinguishes the two clearly. A standing offer is not itself a contract; Canada becomes bound when it issues a call-up, and each call-up is a separate contract. A supply arrangement also does not create a contract at the arrangement stage. Canada becomes bound only when it awards an individual contract under the arrangement, and each such contract is a separate binding contract.

claim_evidence.claim: A standing offer is not itself a contract, and each call-up creates a separate contract.
There is no contractual obligation at the supply arrangement stage.
Each contract awarded under a supply arrangement is a separate binding contract.

question: If a procurement has land claim obligations, do we still have to use the mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement, or can we be exempt?
must_include_concepts: determine whether CLCA obligations apply
mandatory instrument exemption when obligations are not addressed
notification or land-claim handling remains relevant
should_avoid: generic Indigenous procurement language without the mandatory-instrument decision
evaluation_focus: Realistic exemption question that mixes operational and supporting material.
required_claims: When choosing a method of supply, the buyer must determine whether CLCA obligations apply.

If the requirement is subject to CLCA contracting obligations and no mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement addresses those obligations, the client is exempt from using the mandatory instrument.

If a procurement instrument involves CLCA obligations, those obligations must be addressed at the solicitation stage, and for supply arrangements also when issuing solicitations under the arrangement that may be subject to CLCAs.
Any required land claimant group notification or related land-claim handling still has to be carried out.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide does not say CLCA obligations can simply be ignored in favour of a mandatory instrument. The buyer must determine whether CLCA obligations apply. If the requirement is subject to CLCA contracting obligations and no mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement addresses those obligations, the client is exempt from using the mandatory instrument. The applicable CLCA obligations still have to be addressed at the solicitation stage, and land claimant group notification or related CLCA handling remains required where applicable.

claim_evidence.claim: The buyer must determine whether CLCA obligations apply when selecting the method of supply.
If no mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement addresses the applicable CLCA obligations, the client is exempt from using the mandatory instrument.
CLCA obligations must still be addressed at the solicitation stage, and for supply arrangements also for solicitations issued under the arrangement that may be subject to CLCAs.
Land claimant group notification procedures remain relevant where applicable.

question: Do we need to notify land claimant groups for this procurement, and if so how are we supposed to notify them and use the business directories?
must_include_concepts: when the notification obligation arises
methods of notification
business-directory use
should_avoid: broad Indigenous procurement discussion without the notification workflow
evaluation_focus: Operational question a buyer might ask when running a live file.
required_claims: When applicable CLCAs apply, the buyer must notify the relevant land claimant groups.
For procurements posted on GETS, the notice must be sent to the applicable land claimant groups on the posting date and indicate that CLCAs apply.
The buyer may notify by fax or email and should use the applicable business directories or lists to identify beneficiary businesses for notification or invitation.
For procurements not posted on GETS, the buyer must send an equivalent notice and should allow the land claimant groups at least 15 calendar days for inquiries before contract award.
reference_answer: If the applicable CLCAs create a notification obligation, the Buyer’s Guide says the buyer must notify the relevant land claimant groups. For GETS postings, that notice goes out on the posting date and indicates that CLCAs apply. The guide says notification may be by fax or email, and the buyer should use the applicable business directories or lists to identify beneficiary businesses. For procurements not posted on GETS, the buyer must send a notice containing the equivalent information and should allow at least 15 calendar days for inquiries before award.

claim_evidence.claim: Applicable land claimant groups must be notified when the CLCA notification obligation applies.
For GETS procurements, the notice must be sent on the date of posting and indicate that CLCAs apply.
Notification may be by fax or email and the buyer should use the applicable business directories or lists.
For procurements not posted on GETS, the buyer should send an equivalent notice and allow at least 15 calendar days for inquiries before award.

question: For a supply arrangement, if CFTA applies, can we skip publishing an NPP and just use the qualified list? Under exactly what conditions?
must_include_concepts: CFTA qualified-list exception conditions
annual publication of the list of qualified offerors
should_avoid: answering that CFTA always requires an NPP
evaluation_focus: Hard RFSA/CFTA condition-check case.
required_claims: If CFTA applies, you may use the qualified list without publishing an NPP only if all offerors on the source list are invited to submit offers.
Businesses must be able to apply to qualify on the list at any time.
The existence of the list of qualified offerors must still be published annually by an invitation to qualify.
forbidden_claims: CFTA always requires an NPP even when all offerors on the source list are invited and businesses can qualify at any time.
reference_answer: Yes, but only under the Buyer’s Guide conditions for CFTA supply arrangements: all offerors on the source list must be invited to submit offers, businesses must be able to qualify on the list at any time, and the existence of the list still has to be published annually by invitation to qualify.

claim_evidence.claim: The qualified list may be used without publishing an NPP only if all offerors on the source list are invited to submit offers.
Businesses must be able to apply to qualify on the list at any time.
The existence of the list of qualified offerors must still be published annually by invitation to qualify.

notes: Multi-condition answer from a dense RFSA page.

question: What exact form number do I have to use to get ADM approval for a reciprocal procurement exception?
must_include_concepts: ADM or equivalent approval on file for best value or public interest exceptions
insufficient evidence for a specific form number
should_avoid: inventing a specific approval form number
suggesting any PSPC or PWGSC-TPSGC form number as the required reciprocal-procurement approval form
evaluation_focus: Expected-abstain case that should reject fabricated operational details.
required_claims: The provided documents require written ADM or equivalent approval on file for best value or public interest exceptions.
The provided documents do not identify a specific form number for that approval.
forbidden_claims: The provided documents identify a specific form number for ADM approval of a reciprocal procurement exception.
The provided documents identify any PSPC or PWGSC-TPSGC form number as the required form for ADM approval of a reciprocal procurement exception.
reference_answer: The documents say certain reciprocal procurement exceptions require written ADM or equivalent approval on file, but they do not identify a specific form number. The system should say the provided documents are insufficient on that point rather than invent one.

claim_evidence.claim: Best value and public interest reciprocal procurement exceptions require written ADM or equivalent approval on file.
The provided documents do not identify a specific form number for that approval.

notes: Hard failure case for hallucination resistance.

question: I only remember that it was somewhere in the Buyer’s Guide. Which part of the guide should I open if I need the rule about late or delayed offers?
must_include_concepts: Receive and evaluate stage
specific late-or-delayed-offers page
should_avoid: answering with only the generic landing page
evaluation_focus: Navigation-heavy case intended to keep router pages useful without letting them become evidence-heavy.
required_claims: The relevant Buyer’s Guide stage is Receive and evaluate.
The detailed rule is on the Handle late or delayed offers page.
reference_answer: Open the Receive and evaluate part of the Buyer’s Guide, then go to the Handle late or delayed offers page. The landing page only helps you navigate to that detailed operational page.

claim_evidence.claim: The relevant Buyer’s Guide stage is Receive and evaluate.
The detailed rule is on the Handle late or delayed offers page.

notes: Direct probe of router-page usefulness after compact router chunking.

question: The Buy Canadian policy page sounds stricter than the Buyer’s Guide. Can I just rely on the policy page and skip the Buyer’s Guide reciprocal procurement workflow?
must_include_concepts: Buyer’s Guide workflow remains operational source
policy page is supporting context
trade agreement applicability still comes first
should_avoid: treating the policy page as a substitute for the Buyer’s Guide workflow
evaluation_focus: Authority-hierarchy case phrased the way a real buyer might challenge the system.
required_claims: The Buyer’s Guide reciprocal procurement page remains the operational workflow to follow.
The Buy Canadian policy page is supporting context and does not replace the Buyer’s Guide workflow.
Trade agreement applicability should be determined before applying reciprocal procurement rules.
forbidden_claims: A buyer can skip the Buyer’s Guide reciprocal procurement workflow and rely only on the Buy Canadian policy page.
reference_answer: No. The operational workflow still comes from the Buyer’s Guide reciprocal procurement page. The Buy Canadian policy page can provide supporting context, but it does not replace the guide, and the buyer still has to determine trade agreement applicability before applying reciprocal procurement rules.

claim_evidence.claim: The Buyer’s Guide reciprocal procurement page remains the operational workflow to follow.
The Buy Canadian policy page is supporting context and does not replace the Buyer’s Guide workflow.
Trade agreement applicability should be determined before applying reciprocal procurement rules.

notes: Designed to catch policy-over-guide authority drift.

question: Because this file is affected by the new Buy Canadian measures, can we let the supplier send the Canadian content certification after closing within the usual two-business-day cure period, or does it have to come with the offer?
must_include_concepts: general cure-period rule for missing documents
exceptions must be provided at the time of offer submission
Canadian Content certification is an exception
should_avoid: saying the Canadian content certification can always be cured later
treating the high-level Buy Canadian rollout as overriding the Buyer’s Guide verification rule
evaluation_focus: Source-boundary case where the operational timing rule should stay anchored to Verify offers.
required_claims: The Buyer’s Guide says you must not require offerors to provide mandatory certifications, declarations, or proof documents at offer closing unless the requested documentation is included in the Exceptions list.
If required documents are absent at closing, the offeror must be notified and given two business days to submit them, and an offeror who fails to provide them in that timeframe is non-responsive.
The Buyer’s Guide says the Exceptions list covers documents required at the time of offer submission, and Canadian Content Policy certification is one of those exceptions.
forbidden_claims: The Buy Canadian policy page creates a new rule allowing Canadian content certification to be submitted after closing.
reference_answer: It has to come with the offer. The Buyer’s Guide says the normal two-business-day cure period applies only when the missing document is not one of the Exceptions. Canadian Content Policy certification is listed as an exception that is required at the time of offer submission, so the high-level Buy Canadian policy page does not change that workflow rule.

claim_evidence.claim: The Buyer’s Guide says you must not require offerors to provide mandatory certifications, declarations, or proof documents at offer closing unless the requested documentation is included in the Exceptions list.
If required documents are absent at closing, the offeror must be notified and given two business days to submit them, and an offeror who fails to provide them in that timeframe is non-responsive.
The Buyer’s Guide says the Exceptions list covers documents required at the time of offer submission, and Canadian Content Policy certification is one of those exceptions.

question: Where in the Buyer’s Guide do I go to find the rule for minimum solicitation periods for an RFSA or an urgent procurement?
must_include_concepts: Create solicitation navigation
determine the solicitation period page
specific RFSA/urgent minimum-period page
should_avoid: answering with only a number and no navigation route
sending the user to an unrelated solicitation page
evaluation_focus: Navigation case to the specific solicitation-period child page without requiring unnecessary landing-page restatement.
required_claims: The Buyer’s Guide says you should go to Determine the solicitation period and then to Minimum solicitation periods for ITQs, RFSAs and urgent procurements for this rule.
The Determine the solicitation period page says it provides information on the various elements to consider when determining the solicitation period.

The Minimum solicitation periods for ITQs, RFSAs and urgent procurements page contains the RFSA and urgent-procurement rules, including 25 calendar days where one or more international trade agreements apply and 10 calendar days for substantiated urgent procurements where urgency renders the normal solicitation period impracticable.
reference_answer: Go to Determine the solicitation period in the Buyer’s Guide, then open Minimum solicitation periods for ITQs, RFSAs and urgent procurements. That child page is the one with the actual RFSA and urgent-procurement timing rules, including the 25-day and 10-day trade-agreement scenarios.

claim_evidence.claim: The Buyer’s Guide says you should go to Determine the solicitation period and then to Minimum solicitation periods for ITQs, RFSAs and urgent procurements for this rule.
The Determine the solicitation period page says it provides information on the various elements to consider when determining the solicitation period.

The Minimum solicitation periods for ITQs, RFSAs and urgent procurements page contains the RFSA and urgent-procurement rules, including 25 calendar days where one or more international trade agreements apply and 10 calendar days for substantiated urgent procurements where urgency renders the normal solicitation period impracticable.

question: Our file is assessed as medium-high risk and complexity level 3. Which level of delegated authority does the risk-based delegation grid point to, and what actually drives that answer?
must_include_concepts: delegation depends on risk and complexity together
medium-high risk level 3 maps to senior director or equivalent authority
should_avoid: saying approval depends only on dollar value
giving the wrong authority level for medium-high risk level 3
evaluation_focus: Approval-authority lookup case that requires reading the delegation grid correctly rather than answering generically.
required_claims: The risk-based procurement delegation page says delegation limits are determined by both the risk level and the complexity level of the procurement.
For a medium-high risk procurement at complexity level 3, the delegated authority shown in the grid is Senior director, Regional director, or Director.
forbidden_claims: For a medium-high risk procurement at complexity level 3, the delegated authority is ADM.
reference_answer: The delegation decision is driven by the combination of risk level and complexity level. For medium-high risk at complexity level 3, the grid points to Senior director, Regional director, or Director rather than ADM.

claim_evidence.claim: The risk-based procurement delegation page says delegation limits are determined by both the risk level and the complexity level of the procurement.
For a medium-high risk procurement at complexity level 3, the delegated authority shown in the grid is Senior director, Regional director, or Director.

question: Who is supposed to determine the procurement complexity level, and what has to happen if that complexity level changes later in the file?
must_include_concepts: complexity levels range from 1 to 5
manager determines the level
changes must be discussed and documented
should_avoid: saying the buyer alone determines complexity without the manager
omitting the documentation requirement for later changes
evaluation_focus: Approval workflow case about who owns complexity determination and how changes must be documented.
required_claims: The determining complexity page says each requisition undergoes an assessment to determine the procurement's complexity level, and complexity levels range from 1 to 5.
The page says the manager is responsible for determining the complexity of a requirement and the selected complexity level must be documented on file, dated and signed.
The page says any changes to the complexity level during the procurement must be discussed with the manager and documented on file with an explanation of why the change was necessary.
reference_answer: Each requisition must be assessed for complexity on a 1-to-5 scale. The manager determines the complexity level, and the selected level has to be documented on file, dated, and signed. If the level changes later, that change must be discussed with the manager and documented on file with the reason for the change.

claim_evidence.claim: The determining complexity page says each requisition undergoes an assessment to determine the procurement's complexity level, and complexity levels range from 1 to 5.
The page says the manager is responsible for determining the complexity of a requirement and the selected complexity level must be documented on file, dated and signed.
The page says any changes to the complexity level during the procurement must be discussed with the manager and documented on file with an explanation of why the change was necessary.

question: A supplier asked a question during the solicitation period. When can we answer just that supplier directly, when do we need an amendment, and who handles complex technical questions?
must_include_concepts: simple direct response condition
complex questions go to client
when amendment or addendum is required
should_avoid: responding as though every question can be answered privately
evaluation_focus: Live-file solicitation management with branching answer logic.
required_claims: Businesses should submit questions in writing before the deadline stated in the solicitation document.
The buyer must protect the identity of businesses submitting questions.
A simple enquiry may be answered directly to the business asking the question only if the answer will not affect other businesses or how they will respond to the solicitation.
More complex or technical questions about the requirement should be forwarded to the client for response back to the buyer.

Questions and answers should be consolidated into an addendum or amendment and then either published on GETS for publicly advertised solicitations or issued directly to businesses for solicitations that are not publicly advertised.
Changes to the solicitation itself, including any extension to the solicitation period, must be formally incorporated through an amendment to the solicitation.
reference_answer: Questions should come in writing before the solicitation deadline, and the buyer has to protect the questioner’s identity. A simple question can be answered just to the asking supplier only if the answer would not affect other businesses or their responses. More complex or technical requirement questions go to the client for a response back to the buyer. The full Q&A has to be consolidated into an addendum or amendment and then published on GETS if the solicitation is public or sent directly if it is not, and any clarification that changes the solicitation or extends the period must be issued formally by amendment.

claim_evidence.claim: Questions should be submitted in writing before the solicitation deadline.
The identity of businesses submitting questions must be protected.
A simple enquiry may be answered directly only if it will not affect other businesses or their responses.
Complex or technical requirement questions should be forwarded to the client.
Q&A should be consolidated into an amendment or addendum, with publication or direct issuance depending on whether the solicitation is publicly advertised.
Changes to the solicitation, including period extensions, must be formally incorporated through an amendment.

question: In the solicitation, what do I have to say about bid receipt, and when are verbal or direct-to-buyer offers even allowed?
must_include_concepts: method and location of bid receipt must be specified
direct-to-buyer submission threshold
verbal submission limits and urgency condition
unacceptable methods must be stated in solicitation and NPP
should_avoid: saying verbal offers are always acceptable
omitting the solicitation and NPP notice requirement when a method is not acceptable
evaluation_focus: Solicitation drafting case about bid-receipt instructions and allowed submission methods.
required_claims: The offer receipt page says the method and location of bid receipt must be specified in the solicitation of offers.
The page says offers may be submitted directly to the buyer in writing only for offers below $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for construction and services, including all applicable taxes.

The page says verbal submission by telephone is allowed for offers below $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for construction and services, and for any dollar value in cases of documented extreme urgency with the director's approval.
The page says that if a particular method of response is not acceptable, that must be clearly indicated in both the solicitation document and the Notice of Proposed Procurement.
reference_answer: The solicitation has to specify the method and location of bid receipt. Written offers can go directly to the buyer only below the stated dollar thresholds, and verbal submissions by telephone are allowed below those thresholds or at any dollar value only in documented extreme urgency with the director's approval. If a response method is not acceptable, the solicitation and the NPP both have to say so clearly.

claim_evidence.claim: The offer receipt page says the method and location of bid receipt must be specified in the solicitation of offers.
The page says offers may be submitted directly to the buyer in writing only for offers below $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for construction and services, including all applicable taxes.

The page says verbal submission by telephone is allowed for offers below $25,000 for goods and $40,000 for construction and services, and for any dollar value in cases of documented extreme urgency with the director's approval.
The page says that if a particular method of response is not acceptable, that must be clearly indicated in both the solicitation document and the Notice of Proposed Procurement.

question: When does an SRCL have to accompany the requisition, and why would we use the e-SRCL instead of a hard copy?
must_include_concepts: SRCL required when requisition includes a security requirement
requisition section confirms whether security provisions are included
e-SRCL reduces errors and is faster than hard copy
should_avoid: saying every requisition always needs an SRCL regardless of security
omitting the timing advantage of the e-SRCL
evaluation_focus: Security/privacy operational case about when an SRCL is required and why the electronic path matters.
required_claims: The Security Requirements Check List page says all requisitions and requisition amendments for contracts, standing offers, or supply arrangements that include a security requirement must have an accompanying SRCL, statement of work, and relevant security documents.
The page says the client must complete the relevant section on requisition form PWGSC-TPSGC 9200 to confirm whether or not security provisions are included in the requirement.
The page says the online e-SRCL service reduces errors and provides the appropriate security clauses within two working days, compared with 15 working days for hard copies.
reference_answer: An SRCL has to accompany requisitions and requisition amendments for contracts, standing offers, or supply arrangements when the requirement includes security. The client uses the security section of requisition form PWGSC-TPSGC 9200 to confirm whether security provisions are included. The e-SRCL is useful because it reduces errors and gets the appropriate security clauses back in about two working days instead of 15 working days for hard copies.

claim_evidence.claim: The Security Requirements Check List page says all requisitions and requisition amendments for contracts, standing offers, or supply arrangements that include a security requirement must have an accompanying SRCL, statement of work, and relevant security documents.
The page says the client must complete the relevant section on requisition form PWGSC-TPSGC 9200 to confirm whether or not security provisions are included in the requirement.
The page says the online e-SRCL service reduces errors and provides the appropriate security clauses within two working days, compared with 15 working days for hard copies.

question: We cancelled a solicitation and plan to reissue it. What feedback can we give offerors, what do we have to keep out, and when do we have to use both feedback methods?
must_include_concepts: feedback session on reissue
no disclosure of other offers or reissued specifics
TB requires both feedback methods
should_avoid: treating a reissue feedback session like a normal unrestricted debriefing
evaluation_focus: Debriefing and feedback workflow for cancelled-and-reissued solicitations.
required_claims: When a solicitation is cancelled and planned for reissue, a feedback session is available upon request and may include information specific to the offeror’s offer or arrangement, such as which mandatory criteria were passed or failed or the technical score.
The buyer must not disclose details about other offers or arrangements or the specifics of the solicitation of offers to be reissued.

All information provided in a feedback session must be documented by the Contracting Authority, and where relevant it must be provided in generic form in the reissued solicitation to maintain fairness.
Feedback may be delivered by highlighting key areas of concern more prominently in the reissued solicitation and or by organizing an offerors’ conference.
If Treasury Board contract approval is required, both feedback methods must be used.
reference_answer: If the solicitation is being cancelled and reissued, the Buyer’s Guide says a feedback session is available upon request and can give offeror-specific information like failed mandatory criteria or technical score. But you cannot disclose details about other offers or the specifics of the solicitation that will be reissued. The Contracting Authority has to document what was provided and, where relevant, carry it into the reissued solicitation in generic form for fairness. Feedback can be given by emphasizing key concerns in the reissued solicitation, by holding an offerors’ conference, or both, and if Treasury Board approval is required both methods are mandatory.

claim_evidence.claim: A feedback session on a cancelled-and-reissued solicitation may provide offeror-specific information such as failed mandatory criteria or technical score.
Details about other offers and specifics of the solicitation to be reissued must not be disclosed.
Feedback-session information must be documented and, where relevant, provided generically in the reissued solicitation.
Feedback may be provided by highlighting concerns in the reissued solicitation and or by an offerors’ conference, and TB approval requires both methods.

question: What exact form number or file name do I need for the specimen signature card template in the GC network folder when approvals go beyond delegated authority?
must_include_concepts: TB approval beyond delegated authorities
GC-network-only location reference
missing exact form or file identifier
should_avoid: inventing a form number or file name
pretending the page provides downloadable template details
evaluation_focus: Expected-abstain internal-detail case in the approvals-and-authorities family.
required_claims: The page says Treasury Board approval is required for submissions exceeding PSPC’s contracting authorities.

The page says the Delegation of Authorities instrument and Schedule 3 specimen signature card templates can be found in the Delegation of Authorities folder, which is accessible only on the Government of Canada network for PSPC only.
The provided documents do not identify a specific form number or file name for the specimen signature card template.
forbidden_claims: The provided documents identify a specific form number or file name for the specimen signature card template.
reference_answer: The page says approvals beyond those authorities need TB approval and that the Delegation of Authorities instrument plus the Schedule 3 specimen signature card templates are in a GC-network-only folder. But it does not give a specific form number or file name, so the system should say the provided documents are insufficient on that point instead of inventing one.

claim_evidence.claim: The page says Treasury Board approval is required for submissions exceeding PSPC’s contracting authorities.

The page says the Delegation of Authorities instrument and Schedule 3 specimen signature card templates can be found in the Delegation of Authorities folder, which is accessible only on the Government of Canada network for PSPC only.
The provided documents do not identify a specific form number or file name for the specimen signature card template.

notes: Hard failure case for hallucinated internal-form details.

question: We expect repeated, well-defined needs over time, and if we create a standing offer we also need clear rules for ranking and call-ups among multiple suppliers. When does a standing offer make sense, and what has to be built into the RFSO?
must_include_concepts: when a standing offer is appropriate
standing offer is not itself a contract
RFSO ranking and call-up procedure requirements
should_avoid: describing only the benefits of standing offers with no RFSO requirements
treating the standing offer itself as the contract
evaluation_focus: Cross-page synthesis between method-of-supply selection and RFSO document requirements.
required_claims: The Buyer’s Guide says a standing offer is used when goods or services may be required over time using pre-determined conditions and pricing, and a standing offer itself is not a contract because each call-up creates the contract.

The Buyer’s Guide says standing offers are suited where repetitive orders of well-defined goods or services are expected and prices or pricing bases can be established up front without further negotiation at call-up.

The Request for standing offer page says an RFSO must include, at a minimum, clear evaluation criteria and procedures, a clear ranking methodology where applicable, and precise call-up procedures including the work-allocation method among multiple standing offers.
reference_answer: A standing offer makes sense when you expect repeated needs over time for well-defined goods or services and the pricing can be set up front without renegotiating at each call-up. The Guide also says the standing offer itself is not the contract; each call-up creates the contract. If you go that route, the RFSO has to spell out the evaluation criteria, any ranking methodology, and the precise call-up and work-allocation rules among multiple standing offers.

claim_evidence.claim: The Buyer’s Guide says a standing offer is used when goods or services may be required over time using pre-determined conditions and pricing, and a standing offer itself is not a contract because each call-up creates the contract.

The Buyer’s Guide says standing offers are suited where repetitive orders of well-defined goods or services are expected and prices or pricing bases can be established up front without further negotiation at call-up.

The Request for standing offer page says an RFSO must include, at a minimum, clear evaluation criteria and procedures, a clear ranking methodology where applicable, and precise call-up procedures including the work-allocation method among multiple standing offers.
